text: Dr Karim had spent two decades operating on hearts.
His hands were steady, his reputation outstanding, and his schedule relentless.
One evening, after a procedure that lasted nine hours, he sat alone in the hospital car park, unable to move.
The strain of years without rest had finally caught up with him.
A colleague suggested he find an outlet beyond medicine.
On impulse, Karim enrolled in a weekend painting class.
He felt awkward holding a brush — his precise, surgical instincts were useless on canvas.
The instructor told him to stop trying to control the outcome and simply let the colours interact.
The paintings were terrible at first, but something subtle began to shift.
Karim noticed his breathing slowed when he painted.
The tension in his shoulders would ease.
He never became a notable artist, but art gave him what surgery could not — permission to be imperfect.
He still operates five days a week, but now his weekends belong to colour, silence, and the sheer pleasure of making something with no purpose beyond itself.
